title: Truth-Telling and the Right to Know: When Family Asks to Withhold a Diagnosis
subtitle: A 76-year-old woman has newly diagnosed metastatic cancer, and her adult children ask the team not to tell her. The patient is alert and competent and is asking about her results. The nurse must protect her right to know without dismissing the family.

patient.name: R. Adeyemi
patient.admitDate: Medical unit, stable. Newly diagnosed metastatic cancer. Adult children ask the team to withhold the diagnosis. Patient is alert, competent, and asking about her results.

vitals.temp: Ask patient what she wants to know
Nurse lies -> trust broken, autonomy violated

labs.v: Competent adults may know their diagnosis.
How much do you want to know?
labs.r: Some prefer less or defer to family.

labs.v: Can ask family to receive information.
labs.r: The patient's choice, not the family's.

nursesNote: R. Adeyemi, 76, has newly diagnosed metastatic cancer. Her adult children, citing protection and cultural reasons, ask the team not to tell her. She is alert and competent and has been asking about her results. A competent adult has the ethical and legal right to know her own diagnosis and to make informed decisions (autonomy and veracity); the information belongs to the patient, and the nurse must not lie to her or deceive her. At the same time, the nurse asks the patient directly how much she wants to know and whom she wants involved, because a patient may choose to receive less information or designate a family member to hear details — that is the patient's choice, not the family's. The nurse explores the family's concerns with empathy and cultural humility rather than dismissing them, and coordinates a provider-led family meeting where the diagnosis can be disclosed compassionately with support (chaplain, social work, interpreter as needed). If conflict persists, the nurse involves the ethics committee and documents the discussions objectively.
providerOrders: Confirm the patient is decisional and protect her right to information
Ask the patient how much she wishes to know and whom she wants involved
Explore the family's concerns with empathy and cultural humility
Coordinate a provider-led, compassionate disclosure with support services
Involve ethics if conflict persists; document the discussions

opts.text: A competent adult has the right to know her own diagnosis
The health information belongs to the patient, not the family
The nurse should not lie to or deceive the patient
Asking the patient how much she wants to know respects her preferences
The family has the authority to decide what a competent patient is told
The family's concerns should be explored with empathy, not dismissed
A patient may choose to receive less information or designate a family member to hear it
The provider typically delivers the diagnosis; the nurse advocates and supports
Some cultures favor family-centered protective non-disclosure, which can be explored
A family meeting with support services can make disclosure compassionate
Withholding the diagnosis from a competent patient who wants it respects autonomy
If conflict persists, the ethics committee can help
Confirm the patient is competent / decisional
Ask the patient how much she wants to know and whom she wants involved
Explore the family's concerns and cultural context with empathy
Coordinate a provider-led family meeting for disclosure and support
Document and involve ethics if conflict is unresolved

Meet with the family to hear their concerns without dismissing them
